head_coach.name: Robert Saleh
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Pete Carroll/Seattle coaching tree; 4-3 hybrid front with Wide-9 alignment and Cover 3/quarters coverage foundations; aggressive, physical defense that prioritizes front-four pressure over blitzing; evolved from pure Cover 3 to incorporate quarters and split-safety looks

offensive_coordinator.name: Brian Daboll
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Erhardt-Perkins / New England (Belichick/Weis/McDaniels tree), adapted into spread-multiple concepts

defensive_coordinator.name: Gus Bradley
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Pete Carroll/Monte Kiffin 4-3 Under, Cover 3 zone; Seattle Legion of Boom architect

offense.personnel_tendencies: Heavy 11 personnel (historically 65-71% in Buffalo), notable 10 personnel usage (4 WR sets, up to 15% in Buffalo), light 12 personnel usage (8-12%), willing to use 21/12 situationally when roster permits
offense.run_scheme: Multiple: gap scheme foundation (power, counter, duo) married with zone concepts; evolved away from heavy gap toward more zone and pin-and-pull over time; designed QB runs and zone-read integrated
offense.pass_concepts: Play action heavy (led league in PA attempts in Buffalo), bootleg, movement throws, aggressive downfield shots (Bills were 2nd in air yards per attempt 2018-21), RPO integration, mismatch-driven game plans
offense.te_usage: Primarily 11 personnel with TE as versatile piece; Daboll historically used TEs as move pieces (flexed to slot, used as big slot receivers) when talent warrants it; traded for Darren Waller as flex/slot weapon in NYG; TE target share historically low (~12%) in Buffalo but usage increases with receiving TEs; will use 12 personnel more if talent is there but defaults to 3-WR looks
offense.rb_usage: Committee approach with role-based usage; values pass-catching backs for screen game and checkdowns; in Buffalo used Singletary as receiver/pass protector and Moss as ground-pounder; emphasizes RB versatility in pass protection and receiving

defense.alignment: Even front personnel (4 DL) in odd front alignment (Under); features LEO hybrid DE/OLB as primary speed rusher in wide-9 technique, Big End as strong-side run-stuffing DE, nose tackle, 3-technique DT; shifts to Over front in nickel; SAM LB walks down to line in Under creating quasi-3-4 look
defense.edge_rush_source: 4-3 DE hands-in-dirt (LEO position) with hybrid elements; LEO can stand up in 2-point stance in some looks; LEO is essentially a speed rusher (~250 lbs) who aligns in wide-9 technique; Big End is a more traditional run-stopping DE on strong side
defense.coverage_base: Cover 3 primary (historically 43-55% Cover 3 rate under Bradley), evolved to include significant quarters/Cover 4 usage (20%+ under Saleh post-2019), match coverages, Cover 3 bail, some Cover 6; Saleh's latest evolution emphasizes two-deep zone on early downs and man coverage with 5-man rush on third down
defense.blitz_tendency: light (Bradley is historically one of the most conservative blitz callers in the NFL; relies on front-four pressure from LEO and interior DL; will bring occasional zone blitzes and simulated pressures but rarely sends 5+)
defense.lb_role: MIKE and WILL are traditional run-fill and coverage linebackers; SAM is a hybrid who must set the edge against the run and drop into coverage; LBs must be disciplined gap-fillers in Wide-9 scheme since DEs align wide; coverage ability is critical as LBs must react to play-action in this front; Fred Warner-type athletic coverage LBs are the ideal
defense.safety_usage: Cover 3 structure brings strong safety into the box frequently as an 8th run defender; free safety plays deep middle third; Saleh has evolved to use more split-safety looks (quarters) where both safeties play half-field; strong safety must be physical enough to play near the box on early downs but versatile enough to play deep in quarters; scheme creates hybrid box safety role similar to Kam Chancellor archetype

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Daboll's Erhardt-Perkins offense is concept-based and position-interchangeable, allowing TEs to line up anywhere in the formation. He historically used Darren Waller flexed out as a big slot and has deployed move TEs effectively. However, his base offense runs 11 personnel with 3 WRs at a 70%+ rate, keeping TE target share relatively low (~12% in Buffalo). With Cam Ward and a need for weapons, a receiving TE would get moderate value — Daboll will scheme mismatches involving TEs in the slot or flexed wide, but the volume of 3-WR sets limits TE-centric passing. A receiving TE gets a moderate boost because Daboll will feature them creatively, but won't build the offense around the position.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Despite the 4-3 Under front having some 3-4 principles, the primary edge rushers in the Saleh/Bradley system are the LEO and Big End — both classified as defensive ends, not linebackers. The LEO is a hybrid DE/OLB type who can stand up, but is drafted and valued as a DE/EDGE, not a traditional LB. The SAM LB does walk down to the line of scrimmage in Under fronts and has some edge-setting responsibility, but is not the primary pass rusher. The MIKE and WILL LBs are coverage-and-fill players who rarely rush the passer. Bradley's blitz rate is among the lowest in the NFL, meaning off-ball LBs are almost never sent as rushers. A pure LB prospect with pass rush traits does NOT get a significant boost here — this team wants its LBs to cover, fill gaps, and play disciplined, not rush the passer.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: Daboll's offense historically values backs who can contribute in the passing game. In Buffalo, he used Singletary as a pass-catching back and pass protector while Moss handled early-down carries, creating a committee with defined roles. The Erhardt-Perkins system uses screens, checkdowns, and swing routes frequently, and Daboll's play-action heavy approach often creates second-level passing windows where RBs become outlets. With Cam Ward as a mobile QB who may need quick release valves, a dual-threat RB who can catch, run routes, and pass protect would have elevated value. The moderate-to-high boost reflects Daboll's consistent track record of targeting backs in the passing game.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: The Saleh/Bradley Cover 3 system is built on a strong safety/box safety role that is critical to the defense's structure. In Cover 3, the strong safety functions as an 8th man in the box on early downs, responsible for run support, alley fills, and short zone coverage — essentially a linebacker-safety hybrid. The Kam Chancellor archetype is the gold standard for this role: a physical, box-capable safety who can tackle in the run game, cover tight ends, and occasionally drop into deep coverage in quarters looks. Saleh's evolved scheme adds quarters coverage where both safeties play half-field, demanding versatility. A safety with elite tackling, box physicality, slot coverage ability, and blitz capability gets a massive boost in this defense. The scheme literally creates a role where a safety functions like a LB in the box and then plays deep zone — the ultimate hybrid position.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: Tennessee's 2026 draft profile is defined by two scheme identities merging. DEFENSE: Saleh and Bradley's 4-3 Under/Wide-9 system creates extreme positional value at two spots — (1) the LEO edge rusher, a 250-lb speed rusher who aligns in wide-9 and pins his ears back, making smaller, explosive EDGE prospects (even tweener OLB/DE types from college) ideal fits; and (2) the box/strong safety, where the Cover 3 structure demands a Kam Chancellor-type who can function as a quasi-linebacker in run support while covering the deep third in quarters. Off-ball LBs must be athletic coverage players (Fred Warner mold) — pure run-stuffers without coverage range are devalued. Blitz rate is extremely low, so LB pass rush traits are largely irrelevant. OFFENSE: Daboll's Erhardt-Perkins spread system with Cam Ward maximizes QB mobility, play-action, and formation multiplicity. WR value is premium (70%+ in 11/10 personnel). Receiving TEs get moderate value as mismatch weapons when flexed to the slot. Dual-threat RBs who catch and protect are valued over pure runners. The strongest positional value inflection for this team is at safety — draft a box/hybrid safety for Tennessee and he immediately has a defined, high-impact role in this scheme.
